_meta.description: Competitor scan for Nike vs Adidas and Puma

competitors.name: Adidas
competitors.primary_creative_angle: Heritage meets modern street culture. Partnerships with celebrities and musicians. Impossible Is Nothing platform.
competitors.apparent_target_audience: 18-35 year olds into streetwear, sneaker culture, and urban lifestyle. Fashion-forward athletes.
competitors.creative_gaps: Less emotional storytelling than Nike. Product-focused ads can feel transactional. Limited grassroots community engagement.

competitors.name: Puma
competitors.primary_creative_angle: Speed and performance heritage. Bold, colorful aesthetic. Nods to 70s/80s retro sportswear.
competitors.apparent_target_audience: Younger demographic 16-28, value-conscious athletes, motorsport fans, and retro sneaker enthusiasts.
competitors.creative_gaps: Brand lacks a unifying emotional narrative. Marketing often fragmented across sports silos. Less premium perception.

response.category_creative_patterns: All three brands use athlete endorsements heavily. Sustainability messaging is becoming table stakes. Community and belonging themes are rising post-pandemic. Personalization and customization offers are increasingly common.
whitespace_opportunities: Mental health + performance intersection — no brand owns this space authentically
Everyday athlete over elite professional — underrepresented in premium positioning
Local community storytelling at scale — micro-influencers in neighborhood contexts
Transparency in supply chain as creative storytelling, not just CSR fine print
